one_line_takeaway: A simple BM25+vector hybrid retriever eliminates gradient-only GCG-style RAG poisoning (38%->0% co-retrieval, n=50) but is only a partial defense -- an attacker who jointly optimizes for both sparse and dense retrieval channels still achieves 20-44% overall success, and attack/detection difficulty vary sharply by corpus domain (0% success on FEVER Wikipedia vs 11% on a technical Security Stack Exchange corpus, with 13x-62x gaps in detector F1 between corpora).
key_finding: Hybrid BM25+vector retrieval reduced gradient-only poisoning co-retrieval from 38.0% (19/50, pure vector, 95% CI [25.9,51.8]) to 0.0% (0/50) at all three tested hybrid weights (alpha=0.3/0.5/0.7), a statistically significant drop (chi^2=35.8 pooled / 21.05 reported in Figure 2, p<10^-6, Cohen's h=1.33), and 25 adaptive keyword-injection configurations (5 densities x 5 strategies) also all failed (0% co-retrieval). However, an attacker who jointly optimizes for both dense (cosine) and sparse (BM25 overlap) channels partially circumvents this defense, achieving 100% co-retrieval and 20-44% overall success depending on hybrid weight (worst-case for the defender at alpha=0.3: 44% overall success; best case at alpha=0.7: 20%). Across five LLMs (n=15 scenarios each), attack success ranged from 46.7% (GPT-5.3) to 93.3% (Llama 4 Instruct), with safety-violation rates from 6.7% (Claude Sonnet 4.6) to 93.3% (Llama 4); Claude Sonnet 4.6 showed a notable dissociation (60.0% attack success but only 6.7% safety violations and 5.7% payload leakage), attributed by the authors to the model acknowledging retrieved content without acting on it. Corpus composition strongly affects both attack feasibility and detection difficulty: Security SE achieved 66.7% stealth but only 11.1% overall attack success (n=9) / 6.0% overall success (n=50, Table 5), while FEVER Wikipedia achieved 100% co-retrieval but 0% stealth and 0% overall success across all 25 confirmatory trials and all retrieval configurations (Table 4). Detection-method F1 scores varied 13x-62x between the two corpora (Table 10: keyword anomaly 0.400 on FEVER vs 0.010 on Security SE, a 40x gap; clustering 0.182 vs 0.003, 62x gap), with QPD showing the smallest cross-corpus gap (0.632 vs 0.171, 3.7x) and thus judged the most corpus-robust detector. The production case study (156,777 vendor docs) found naive cross-corpus attacks achieved 0% retrieval (0/3 scenarios) while corpus-adaptive attacks (using the target embedding model) achieved 100% retrieval (3/3) at rank #1-2 with cosine similarity 0.744-0.785.
core_contribution: The paper's own primary contribution (explicitly labeled 'primary contribution' in the text) is an empirical demonstration that a simple hybrid BM25 + dense-vector retriever acts as an architectural defense against gradient-guided (GCG-style) RAG poisoning: across n=50 attacks on a Security Stack Exchange corpus (67,941 docs), hybrid retrieval reduced gradient-guided co-retrieval success from 38.0% (pure vector) to 0% at all tested mixing weights (alpha=0.3, 0.5, 0.7). The authors also contribute (2) a multi-model end-to-end evaluation of the full poisoning attack chain across five LLMs from four providers, showing attack success ranging 46.7%-93.3% and safety-violation rates 6.7%-93.3%; (3) an exploratory characterization of corpus-dependent attack/detection dynamics comparing a technical corpus (Security SE) against a general corpus (FEVER Wikipedia, n=9 pilot and n=25 confirmation), finding 13x-62x differences in detection-method F1 gap between corpora; and (4) a preliminary production-scale case study on a 156,777-document vendor security corpus showing naive (cross-corpus) attacks fail completely (0%) while corpus-adaptive attacks reach 100% retrieval with rank #1-2. The paper explicitly states the attack technique itself (GCG-style dual-document sleeper-trigger poisoning) is not novel -- it builds on existing PoisonedRAG-style optimization and GCG (cited as [14] and [13]) -- and frames its contribution as the defense evaluation and cross-model/cross-corpus empirical analysis, not a new attack algorithm.
threat_model: Defined explicitly in Section 3.1. Attacker capabilities: can inject documents into the RAG corpus (via user-generated content, API submissions, or compromised data pipelines), knows target query patterns and the embedding model (or a proxy), but cannot modify LLM parameters, system prompts, or retrieval algorithms. Attack class scope is limited to (1) gradient-guided semantic optimization (GCG-style coordinate search maximizing embedding cosine similarity) and (2) joint sparse+dense optimization (simultaneously targeting vector similarity and BM25 keyword overlap to circumvent hybrid retrieval); black-box search methods (genetic algorithms, RL) are explicitly out of scope and left as future work. Success is measured via four defined metrics: co-retrieval (both sleeper and trigger documents appear in top-k for the target query), any-retrieval, stealth (<30% retrieval rate on benign queries), and overall success (co-retrieval AND stealth). Defender capabilities: controls retrieval architecture (pure vector, hybrid, or keyword-based), can analyze retrieval patterns/embedding distributions and has query logs, but cannot prevent initial document injection -- i.e., this is a detection-and-mitigation-focused threat model, not a prevention-of-injection model.
limitations: Joint sparse+dense optimization already achieves 20-44% success against the paper's headline hybrid-retrieval defense; the authors note untested black-box attack methods (genetic algorithms, RL) could potentially do better. Cross-corpus comparison pilot uses only n=9 per corpus; while FEVER is confirmed at n=25, Security SE generalization beyond n=50 is left as future work. GPT-5.3 was evaluated at temperature=1.0 (API-mandated) while all other models used temperature=0.3, which the authors flag as a potential confound on cross-model attack-success comparability. The production vendor case study covers only 3 scenarios and is explicitly called 'illustrative rather than conclusive.' Retrieval-quality impact of hybrid weighting (the quality/security trade-off at alpha<=0.5) is not empirically measured; the recommended alpha<=0.5 setting is estimated from related work, not validated in this paper. The 30% stealth threshold used throughout is described by the authors as 'deliberately generous'; production systems would likely use stricter (<5%) thresholds. The attack technique (GCG-style dual-document poisoning) is not novel; the authors state their contribution is the defense evaluation and cross-model/cross-corpus analysis rather than a new attack algorithm. Corpus generalization is limited to two domains (technical Q&A and general encyclopedic); the authors explicitly note broader domains (medical, legal, financial) remain future work.

datasets_used: Security Stack Exchange (technical Q&A corpus, 67,941 documents, OpenAI text-embedding-3-small 1536d embeddings, ChromaDB cosine-similarity vector store)
FEVER Wikipedia (general encyclopedic corpus, 96,561 articles; large-scale confirmation phase used a 2,000-document representative sample; same embedding/retrieval configuration as Security SE)
Production vendor documentation corpus (156,777 documents from an unnamed major network security vendor, all-MiniLM-L6-v2 384d embeddings, ChromaDB)
quantitative_results: Pure vector retrieval: 38.0% co-retrieval (19/50), 72.0% any-retrieval (36/50), 62.0% stealth (31/50), 6.0% overall success (3/50) on Security SE (n=50, top-5, Table 5)

Hybrid retrieval (alpha=0.3/0.5/0.7) vs gradient-only attacks: 0.0% co-retrieval (0/50), 100% stealth (50/50), 0.0% overall success at every alpha (Table 6); chi^2=35.8 pooled (Fig.2 reports chi^2=21.05), p<10^-6, Cohen's h=1.33

Joint sparse+dense attack on hybrid retrieval (n=25): 100% co-retrieval at all alpha, overall success 20% (alpha=0.7), 36% (alpha=0.5), 44% (alpha=0.3); mean dense similarity 0.81-0.84, mean BM25 overlap 1.00, mean benign retrieval rate 0.39-0.54 (Table 7)
Adaptive keyword injection defense test: all 25 configurations (5 densities x 5 strategies) achieved 0% co-retrieval
Cross-corpus pilot (n=9, top-10, Table 2): Security SE 44.4% co-retrieval / 66.7% stealth / 11.1% overall; FEVER 100% co-retrieval / 0% stealth / 0% overall
FEVER large-scale confirmation (n=25, Table 4): 100% co-retrieval, 0% stealth, 0% overall across pure vector and all hybrid configs at both top-5 and top-10; mean benign retrieval rates 0.92-1.00

Multi-model end-to-end evaluation (n=15/model, Table 8): attack success/safety-violation%/leakage%/output-divergence -- GPT-5.3 46.7%/33.3%/9.6%/0.284; GPT-4o 53.3%/86.7%/12.0%/0.483; GPT-4o-mini 53.3%/86.7%/14.9%/0.418; Claude Sonnet 4.6 60.0%/6.7%/5.7%/0.196; Llama 4 Instruct 93.3%/93.3%/56.8%/0.268

Production case study (Table 9): naive cross-corpus attacks 0% retrieval (0/3); corpus-adaptive attacks 100% retrieval (3/3), avg similarity 0.761, rank #1-2 (VPN bypass 0.785, firewall troubleshooting 0.756, endpoint detection false positives 0.744)

Detection method F1 comparison (Table 10): Keyword Anomaly 0.400 (FEVER) vs 0.010 (Sec SE), 40x gap; Semantic Drift 0.182 vs 0.014, 13x gap; QPD 0.632 vs 0.171, 3.7x gap; Clustering 0.182 vs 0.003, 62x gap

Detection operating points at fixed FPR (Table 11): QPD at FPR=0.01% gives 6.8 alerts/day, 16.7% recall on Sec SE and 9.7 alerts/day, 66.7% recall on FEVER; Keyword anomaly at FPR=0.1% gives 68 alerts/day, 66.7% recall on Sec SE and 97 alerts/day, 100% recall on FEVER
Holdout validation of QPD: FEVER train/test gap <0.01, Security SE gap = 0.000
Attack category breakdown within Security SE n=50 set: baseline 37.5%, advanced 35.3%, stealth-optimized 41.2% co-retrieval (chi^2=0.14, p=0.93, no significant difference)
baselines_compared: Pure vector (dense-only) retrieval vs hybrid BM25+vector retrieval at alpha=0.3/0.5/0.7 (primary architectural comparison)
Gradient-only GCG attack vs joint sparse+dense GCG attack (attack-side comparison against the hybrid defense)
Five LLMs as generation backends: GPT-5.3, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Llama 4 Instruct

Four detection methods compared head-to-head: keyword anomaly (TF-IDF rarity), semantic drift (embedding-centroid distance), query pattern differential (QPD), and clustering-based co-retrieval detection

Related-work baselines cited but not re-run: PoisonedRAG [14] (90% attack success on standard QA benchmarks), RAG Security Bench [11] (13 poisoning methods x 5 datasets x 7 defenses), RAGuard [6] fragment-wise perplexity filtering (cited as showing limited effectiveness on technical corpora) -- these are Related Work comparisons, not experiments run by this paper
